pullRequest.title: SSE Integration  for MCP Server and file refactoring
pullRequest.body:
Closes  #5 

## Task:  Added SSE integration

User now will have an option to host the server using two modes, 

1. using setup : `python main.py --setup` hosts through sse
2. using --no-setup :  `python main.py --no-setup` hosts through stdio

Based on the option, the mcp server will be hosted in that mode.

<details>
<summary>1. std-io mode RESPONSE</summary>

```
.
.
Choose transport mode:
1. stdio (default)
3. sse (Server-Sent Events)
Enter 1 or 2 [1]: 1

🚀 Running LinkedIn MCP server (STDIO mode)...
```
</details>


<details>
<summary>2. sse mode RESPONSE</summary>

```
.
.
Choose transport mode:
1. stdio (default)
2. sse (Server-Sent Events)
Enter 1 or 2 [1]: 2

🚀 Running LinkedIn MCP server (SSE mode)...
INFO:     Started server process [17355]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
```
</details>

pullRequest.title: feat: add configurable user agent support
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
- Add `--user-agent` CLI argument and `USER_AGENT` environment variable support
- Implement platform-specific default user agents (Windows, macOS, Linux)
- Update configuration schema and loaders to support user agent customization
- Add user agent documentation to readme

## Addresses Issues
- Closes #27: Option to set useragent
- Should help with #26: Cookie authentication fails on Windows

## Changes
- **Configuration**: Added `user_agent` field to `ChromeConfig` schema
- **CLI**: Added `--user-agent` argument with help text
- **Environment**: Added `USER_AGENT` environment variable support
- **Platform Detection**: Automatic platform-specific user agents to reduce fingerprinting
- **Documentation**: Updated README.md

pullRequest.title: Fix UnicodeEncodeError on Windows by enforcing UTF-8 output
pullRequest.body:
### Description

This pull request fixes the `UnicodeEncodeError` that occurs when printing emoji characters on Windows systems with `cp1252` encoding by enforcing UTF-8 output encoding in the console.

### Changes Made

- Added explicit UTF-8 encoding enforcement for Windows console output.
- Updated relevant print statements to handle emojis without errors.
- Added a platform check to ensure the fix only applies on Windows.

### Related Issue

Closes #29

### Testing

Tested on Windows 10 and confirmed that emoji characters print correctly without raising `UnicodeEncodeError`.

pullRequest.title: fix(deps): upgrade fastmcp to fix 307 redirect issue
pullRequest.body:
Upgrade fastmcp from >=2.10.1 to >=2.14.0 to fix the 307 Temporary
Redirect issue when using streamable-http transport.

The fix was merged in FastMCP PR #896 and #998, which changed default
paths to include trailing slashes and removed automatic path
manipulation that caused redirect loops with Starlette's Mount routing.

This also upgrades mcp from 1.10.1 to 1.25.0 which includes related
fixes confirmed by users in modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk#1168.

Resolves: #54

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> Upgrades core MCP stack and refreshes lockfile to resolve HTTP redirect issues and align with upstream API changes.
> 
> - Bumps `fastmcp` to `>=2.14.0` and `mcp` to `1.25.0` (addresses 307 redirect behavior per upstream changes)
> - Regenerates `uv.lock` with many transitive updates and additions required by new `fastmcp`/`mcp` (e.g., `cyclopts`, `jsonschema-path`, `platformdirs`, `py-key-value-aio` + extras, `pydocket`, `websockets`, OpenTelemetry packages, `redis`), plus assorted version bumps (`selenium`, `urllib3`, `uvicorn`, `trio`, `certifi`)
> - No source code changes outside dependency/version metadata
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit f2b67c21437afab6bcd83aa5aa026a7a1cd264de. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

pullRequest.title: fix(dxt): use ${HOME} instead of tilde in Docker volume mount
pullRequest.body:
Replace ~ with ${HOME} in manifest.json Docker volume mount path.
Tilde expansion only works in shell context, not when Docker is
invoked directly by Claude Desktop or DXT extension.

Resolves: #87

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> Updates Docker invocation config for reliability outside shell contexts.
> 
> - In `manifest.json`, switches volume mount from `~/.linkedin-mcp` to `${HOME}/.linkedin-mcp` in `docker run` args

pullRequest.title: fix(deps): update linkedin-scraper to 3.1.1 to fix --get-session hang
pullRequest.body:
Updates linkedin-scraper from >=3.1.0 to >=3.1.1 which includes a fix for
authentication detection that was causing --get-session to hang indefinitely.

The upstream fix (joeyism/linkedin_scraper@55f2305) improves is_logged_in()
to handle LinkedIn's A/B tested DOM variants by:
- Adding URL-based fallback detection
- Checking multiple nav selector patterns
- Failing fast on auth blocker URLs

Resolves: #95
Related: joeyism/linkedin_scraper#269

pullRequest.title: feat(tools): add posts section to get_person_profile
pullRequest.body:
Closes #164 Adds `posts` as a new section option for `get_person_profile`, allowing users to retrieve recent activity from a person's LinkedIn profile. Follows the exact same pattern as all existing person sections — added a `POSTS` flag to `PersonScrapingFields`, wired it into the `page_map` in `scrape_person()` with `/recent-activity/all/` as the target URL, and updated `PERSON_SECTION_MAP` so users can request it via `sections="posts"`. ## Changes - `scraping/fields.py` — added `POSTS` flag to `PersonScrapingFields` and `"posts"` entry to `PERSON_SECTION_MAP` - `scraping/extractor.py` — added `POSTS` tuple to `page_map` in `scrape_person()` - `tools/person.py` — updated docstring to include `posts` in available sections - `AGENTS.md` — updated `get_person_profile` tool description - `tests/test_fields.py` — updated `test_all_sections` to include `POSTS` flag - `tests/test_scraping.py` — updated `test_all_flags_visit_all_pages` and added `test_posts_visits_recent_activity`

Greptile Summary

This PR adds `posts` as a new optional section for `get_person_profile`, scraping a person's LinkedIn recent-activity feed at `/in/{username}/recent-activity/all/`. The implementation is minimal and consistent — a new `POSTS` flag in `PersonScrapingFields`, a single tuple added to `scrape_person`'s `page_map`, and a map entry in `PERSON_SECTION_MAP` — exactly mirroring how every other person section (experience, education, etc.) is wired in. Key changes: - `scraping/fields.py`: `POSTS = auto()` added to `PersonScrapingFields`; `"posts"` entry added to `PERSON_SECTION_MAP` - `scraping/extractor.py`: `(PersonScrapingFields.POSTS, "posts", "/recent-activity/all/", False)` tuple added to `page_map` in `scrape_person()` - `tools/person.py` / `AGENTS.md` / `README.md` / `docs/docker-hub.md`: documentation updated consistently across all surfaces - `tests/test_fields.py` & `tests/test_scraping.py`: new flag added to existing tests; dedicated `test_posts_visits_recent_activity` test added

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it is a small, additive change that follows the existing codebase pattern exactly with no modifications to shared logic. - The change is purely additive: one new enum value, one new `page_map` tuple, one new map entry, and corresponding doc/test updates. It does not touch any shared scraping logic, error handling, or authentication flow. All previously-flagged review concerns (README update, URL path specificity in tests, `asyncio.sleep` patching) have either been addressed or explicitly deferred to a separate issue by the team. - No files require special attention.

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | linkedin_mcp_server/scraping/fields.py | Adds `POSTS = auto()` to `PersonScrapingFields` and `"posts": PersonScrapingFields.POSTS` to `PERSON_SECTION_MAP`. Clean, minimal, follows the established flag pattern exactly. | | linkedin_mcp_server/scraping/extractor.py | Adds a `(PersonScrapingFields.POSTS, "posts", "/recent-activity/all/", False)` entry to the `page_map` list inside `scrape_person()`. Correctly sets `is_overlay=False` (regular page, not a dialog) and the URL suffix matches the live LinkedIn activity feed path. | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/person.py | Docstring updated to list `posts` in the available sections and add a usage example. No logic changes. | | tests/test_scraping.py | Updates `test_all_flags_visit_all_pages` to include `POSTS` (count bumped from 7 to 8, section name added) and adds a focused `test_posts_visits_recent_activity` test. Neither test patches `asyncio.sleep`, so each navigation adds a real 2 s delay — a pre-existing issue tracked in a separate issue. | | tests/test_fields.py | Adds `PersonScrapingFields.POSTS` to `test_atomic_flags_are_distinct` and `test_all_sections`. Both updates are correct and complete. | | AGENTS.md | Tool description updated to include `posts` in the `get_person_profile` sections list. Consistent with the README update. | | README.md | Tool description in the tools table updated to include `posts`. Addresses the gap called out in the previous review round. | | docs/docker-hub.md | Adds a "Person Posts" bullet to the features list. Minor doc update, no issues. |

Sequence Diagram

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AI as AI Assistant participant Tool as get_person_profile participant Parser as parse_person_sections participant Extractor as LinkedInExtractor participant LI as LinkedIn AI->>Tool: sections="posts" Tool->>Parser: parse_person_sections("posts") Parser-->>Tool: BASIC_INFO | POSTS, unknown=[] Tool->>Extractor: scrape_person(username, BASIC_INFO|POSTS) Extractor->>LI: GET /in/{username}/ LI-->>Extractor: main profile page text Note over Extractor: asyncio.sleep(2s) Extractor->>LI: GET /in/{username}/recent-activity/all/ LI-->>Extractor: recent activity page text Note over Extractor: asyncio.sleep(2s) Extractor-->>Tool: {url, sections:{main_profile, posts}, pages_visited, sections_requested} Tool-->>AI: result dict ``` Last reviewed commit: 7c7eb22

Confidence Score: 4/5

All added tools and features are correctly documented and exist in the code. The only remaining note is a minor UX/discoverability observation about how Person Posts is accessed differently than Company Posts, which doesn't affect functionality but could be clearer in documentation. - No files require special attention — the manifest accurately reflects the implementation. Last reviewed commit: 777f9de

pullRequest.title: refactor(scraping): replace Flag enums with config dicts
pullRequest.body:
Collapse three parallel data structures (Flag enums, SECTION_MAP dicts, page_map lists) into single PERSON_SECTIONS/COMPANY_SECTIONS config dicts. Remove pages_visited and sections_requested from return format. Resolves: #181

Greptile Summary

This PR collapses three parallel data structures (Flag enums, `SECTION_MAP` dicts, and inline `page_map` lists) into a single `PERSON_SECTIONS` / `COMPANY_SECTIONS` config dict per domain, significantly reducing the surface area a contributor must touch when adding a new section. It also removes `pages_visited` and `sections_requested` from all return formats, simplifying the public API. Key changes: - **`fields.py`**: Flag enums and section-map dicts replaced by `dict[str, tuple[str, bool]]` config dicts; parse functions now validate against the full config (including baseline keys), correctly handling explicit `"main_profile"` / `"about"` inputs. - **`extractor.py`**: `scrape_person` and `scrape_company` now iterate the config dicts directly, accept `set[str]`, properly dispatch `is_overlay` in `scrape_company`, and guard the navigation delay with a `first` flag to avoid a wasted trailing sleep. - **Tests**: All tests updated to use the new `set[str]` API, derive URL assertions from `mock.call_args_list`, and patch `asyncio.sleep` in every test method that exercises the scrape loop. - **Docs** (`AGENTS.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`): Architecture guide, contributor checklist, and return-format documentation all updated to reflect the new design. All issues raised in previous review rounds have been addressed in this PR.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it is a clean, self-contained refactor with no functional regressions and complete test coverage. - All previously-raised issues (trailing delay, `is_overlay` ignored in `scrape_company`, baseline section treated as unknown, missing `asyncio.sleep` patches, incomplete return-format docs) are correctly resolved. The new config-dict design is simpler and logically equivalent to the Flag + map approach it replaces. Tests cover baseline inclusion, overlay dispatch, error isolation, unknown sections, and URL routing for every section. - No files require special attention.

Flowchart

```mermaid %%{init: {'theme': 'neutral'}}%% flowchart TD A["Tool call\nget_person_profile(username, sections)"] --> B["parse_person_sections(sections)"] B --> C{"name in\nPERSON_SECTIONS?"} C -- Yes --> D["requested.add(name)"] C -- No --> E["unknown.append(name)\nlog warning"] D --> F["scrape_person(username, requested)"] E --> F F --> G["requested = requested | {'main_profile'}"] G --> H["for section_name, (suffix, is_overlay)\nin PERSON_SECTIONS.items()"] H --> I{"section_name\nin requested?"} I -- No --> H I -- Yes --> J{"first?"} J -- No --> K["await asyncio.sleep(_NAV_DELAY)"] J -- Yes --> L["first = False\nurl = base_url + suffix"] K --> L L --> M{"is_overlay?"} M -- Yes --> N["_extract_overlay(url)"] M -- No --> O["extract_page(url)"] N --> P{"text?"} O --> P P -- Yes --> Q["sections[section_name] = text"] P -- No --> H Q --> H H -- done --> R["return {url, sections}"] R --> S{"unknown?"} S -- Yes --> T["result['unknown_sections'] = unknown"] S -- No --> U["Return result to caller"] T --> U ``` Last reviewed commit: 0c0e125

pullRequest.title: refactor(tools): Use Depends() to inject extractor
pullRequest.body:
Replace repeated ensure_authenticated/get_or_create_browser/ LinkedInExtractor boilerplate in all 6 tool functions with FastMCP Depends()-based dependency injection via a single get_extractor() factory in dependencies.py. Resolves: #186

Greptile Summary

This PR eliminates the repeated `ensure_authenticated` / `get_or_create_browser` / `LinkedInExtractor` boilerplate across all six tool functions by introducing a single `get_extractor()` async factory in `dependencies.py`, injected into each tool via `Depends(get_extractor)`. The refactor is clean, reduces duplication, and correctly handles errors in the DI layer — `get_extractor()` now has its own `try/except` that routes exceptions through `raise_tool_error()` before they can escape FastMCP's dependency resolution phase. - **New `dependencies.py`** — centralises browser acquisition, authentication, and extractor construction in one place; errors are converted to `ToolError` via `raise_tool_error()`. - **All three tool modules** (`person.py`, `company.py`, `job.py`) — `~6 lines of boilerplate removed per function`; logic is entirely unchanged. - **`test_tools.py`** — test setup is simplified (mock extractor now injected directly as a kwarg); a new `test_get_person_profile_auth_error` test verifies DI-layer auth failures produce proper `ToolError` responses via `mcp.call_tool()`. - **`Depends` is imported from `fastmcp.dependencies`** (an internal submodule) in all three tool files and `dependencies.py` — worth checking whether a stable public import path exists. - **DI-layer regression test coverage** is limited to `get_person_profile`; the remaining five tools have no equivalent `mcp.call_tool()`-based test to catch accidental removal of their `Depends` wiring.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge — refactor is logically correct and DI-layer error handling is now explicitly tested, with only minor maintainability concerns remaining. - The core refactor is sound: boilerplate is properly centralised, `get_extractor()` guards its own error path, and a new end-to-end DI test was added to address the key correctness concern from prior reviews. The two remaining points are style/maintenance: the `Depends` import from an internal FastMCP submodule could break on a FastMCP upgrade, and the DI-layer auth regression test covers only one of the six tools. - `linkedin_mcp_server/dependencies.py` (double `get_or_create_browser` call, `Depends` import path) and `tests/test_tools.py` (DI-layer test coverage limited to one tool).

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | linkedin_mcp_server/dependencies.py | New DI factory centralising browser acquisition and authentication; correctly wraps errors via raise_tool_error(), but get_or_create_browser() is called twice per request (once here, once inside ensure_authenticated()) — a pre-existing inefficiency now visible in one place. | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/person.py | Clean removal of repeated boilerplate; extractor injected via Depends(); tool logic unchanged. | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/company.py | Same boilerplate removal as person.py; both get_company_profile and get_company_posts now receive the extractor via Depends(). | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/job.py | Same boilerplate removal as person.py and company.py; no logic changes. | | tests/test_tools.py | Tests simplified by injecting mock extractor directly; new test_get_person_profile_auth_error exercises the DI-layer error path end-to-end, but the equivalent DI-layer test only exists for get_person_profile — the other five tools are not covered. |

Sequence Diagram

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as MCP Client participant FastMCP as FastMCP Router participant DI as Depends(get_extractor) participant Dep as dependencies.py
get_extractor() participant Browser as browser.py participant Tool as Tool Function
(person/company/job) participant Extractor as LinkedInExtractor Client->>FastMCP: call_tool("get_person_profile", args) FastMCP->>DI: resolve dependencies DI->>Dep: await get_extractor() Dep->>Browser: await get_or_create_browser() Browser-->>Dep: browser (singleton) Dep->>Browser: await ensure_authenticated() alt Auth failure Browser-->>Dep: raise AuthenticationError / SessionExpiredError Dep->>Dep: catch Exception e Dep->>Dep: raise_tool_error(e, "get_extractor") Dep-->>FastMCP: raise ToolError FastMCP-->>Client: ToolError response else Auth success Browser-->>Dep: ok Dep-->>DI: LinkedInExtractor(browser.page) DI-->>FastMCP: extractor resolved FastMCP->>Tool: call with (args..., extractor=extractor) Tool->>Extractor: await extractor.scrape_*(...) alt Scrape failure Extractor-->>Tool: raise Exception Tool->>Tool: except Exception e Tool->>Tool: raise_tool_error(e, "tool_name") Tool-->>FastMCP: raise ToolError FastMCP-->>Client: ToolError response else Scrape success Extractor-->>Tool: result dict Tool-->>FastMCP: return result FastMCP-->>Client: success response end end ``` Last reviewed commit: 5bf48f4

pullRequest.title: feat(tools): add global 90s tool timeouts
pullRequest.body:
## Summary - add FastMCP tool timeouts of `90.0` seconds to all 7 MCP tools - add a tool metadata test to assert `timeout == 90.0` for each tool - document the distinction between FastMCP tool execution timeout and browser `TIMEOUT` config ## Validation - `uv run pytest tests/test_tools.py` - `uv run ruff check .` - `uv run ty check` Resolves #187

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces a centralized `TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 90.0` constant and applies it as a FastMCP `timeout` parameter to all 7 registered MCP tools (`get_person_profile`, `search_people`, `get_company_profile`, `get_company_posts`, `get_job_details`, `search_jobs`, `close_session`). A `TestToolTimeouts` test class is also added to assert each tool carries the expected timeout metadata. - **New `constants.py`**: Clean, single-responsibility module for project-wide constants; avoids magic numbers scattered across tool files. - **Tool files (`person.py`, `company.py`, `job.py`, `server.py`)**: Each imports `TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` and passes it to `@mcp.tool(timeout=...)` — a straightforward, low-risk change. - **Test coverage**: `test_all_tools_have_global_timeout` verifies the timeout metadata on every registered tool using `await mcp.get_tool(name)`, which is the correct public FastMCP API. - **Minor style issue**: In `server.py` the `constants` import was inserted before `authentication`, breaking the alphabetical ordering used by the rest of the first-party imports.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge; the change is minimal, well-tested, and does not alter any tool logic. - All 7 tools are correctly decorated, the constant is properly centralized, and a dedicated test class verifies the metadata contract. The only finding is a minor import ordering issue in `server.py` that does not affect runtime behavior. - No files require special attention beyond the minor import ordering issue in `server.py`.

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | linkedin_mcp_server/constants.py | New file introducing the centralized `TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 90.0` constant; clean and correct. | | linkedin_mcp_server/server.py | Adds `timeout=TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` to `close_session`; minor import ordering issue (`constants` inserted before `authentication`, breaking alphabetical order). | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/company.py | Correctly applies `timeout=TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` to both `get_company_profile` and `get_company_posts`. | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/job.py | Correctly applies `timeout=TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` to both `get_job_details` and `search_jobs`. | | linkedin_mcp_server/tools/person.py | Correctly applies `timeout=TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` to both `get_person_profile` and `search_people`. | | tests/test_tools.py | Adds `TestToolTimeouts` class with a metadata test asserting `tool.timeout == TOOL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` for all 7 registered tools; comprehensive and correct coverage. |

Flowchart

```mermaid %%{init: {'theme': 'neutral'}}%% flowchart TD A[MCP Client calls tool] --> B{FastMCP runtime} B -->|starts timer| C[Tool execution begins] C --> D{Elapsed < 90s?} D -- Yes --> E[Tool completes normally] D -- No --> F[TimeoutError raised] E --> G[Return result to client] F --> H[raise_tool_error propagates ToolError] H --> G subgraph Tools ["All 7 registered tools (timeout=90.0s)"] T1[get_person_profile] T2[search_people] T3[get_company_profile] T4[get_company_posts] T5[get_job_details] T6[search_jobs] T7[close_session] end B -.->|dispatches to| Tools ``` Last reviewed commit: fed0ace

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `linkedin_mcp_server/server.py`, line 14-16 ([link](https://github.com/stickerdaniel/linkedin-mcp-server/blob/fed0ace14dc7f192f34182620f7d21428ddc1d98/linkedin_mcp_server/server.py#L14-L16)) **Import order breaks alphabetical convention** The new `constants` import is placed before `authentication`, but alphabetically `authentication` < `constants` (i.e., `'a' < 'c'`). This breaks the isort ordering used by the rest of the file. While `ruff check` may not catch this if the `I` (isort) rule set isn't enabled, it is inconsistent with how the remaining `linkedin_mcp_server.*` imports are ordered. Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

pullRequest.title: refactor(tools): Simplify annotations to dict syntax and add tags
pullRequest.body:
Replace ToolAnnotations(...) with plain dicts, move title to top-level @mcp.tool() param, and add category tags to all tools. Resolves: #189

Greptile Summary

This PR is a clean, well-scoped refactoring that modernises tool metadata across all four changed files to align with the FastMCP 3.x API. It introduces no functional or behavioural changes. Key changes: - Removes the `ToolAnnotations(...)` Pydantic wrapper in `company.py`, `job.py`, and `person.py`, replacing it with plain `dict` syntax for the `annotations` parameter — the simpler form supported by FastMCP 3.x. - Moves `title` from inside `ToolAnnotations` to a top-level keyword argument on `@mcp.tool()`, matching the updated FastMCP 3.x decorator signature. - Drops the now-redundant `destructiveHint=False` from all read-only tools. Per the MCP spec, `destructiveHint` is only meaningful when `readOnlyHint` is `false`, so omitting it from tools that already declare `readOnlyHint=True` is semantically equivalent. - Adds `tags` (as Python `set` literals) to every tool for categorisation (`"company"`, `"job"`, `"person"`, `"scraping"`, `"search"`, `"session"`). - Enriches the previously unannotated `close_session` tool in `server.py` with a title, `destructiveHint=True`, and the `"session"` tag — accurately describing its destructive nature. The existing test suite in `tests/test_tools.py` covers all tool functions but does not assert on annotation metadata, so no test changes are required. The refactoring is consistent across all tool files and fits naturally within the project's layered registration pattern.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it is a pure metadata/annotation refactoring with no changes to tool logic, inputs, outputs, or error handling. - All changes are limited to decorator parameters (`title`, `annotations`, `tags`). The `annotations` dict values are semantically equivalent to the removed `ToolAnnotations` objects, `destructiveHint=False` is correctly dropped only for `readOnlyHint=True` tools, and the new `close_session` annotations accurately reflect its destructive nature. No business logic, scraping behaviour, or error paths were altered. - No files require special attention.

Flowchart

```mermaid %%{init: {'theme': 'neutral'}}%% flowchart TD A["@mcp.tool() decorator"] --> B{Annotation style} B -->|Before| C["ToolAnnotations(title=..., readOnlyHint=..., destructiveHint=False, openWorldHint=...)"] B -->|After| D["title='...' (top-level param)\nannotations={'readOnlyHint': True, 'openWorldHint': True}\ntags={'category', 'type'}"] D --> E["person tools\n(get_person_profile, search_people)"] D --> F["company tools\n(get_company_profile, get_company_posts)"] D --> G["job tools\n(get_job_details, search_jobs)"] D --> H["session tool\n(close_session)\nannotations={'destructiveHint': True}"] ``` Last reviewed commit: c5bf554

Lock file already has 3.1.0 since #166; align pyproject.toml floor to prevent accidental downgrades to v2. Resolves: #190

Greptile Summary

This PR tightens the `fastmcp` minimum version constraint from `>=2.14.0` to `>=3.0.0` in `pyproject.toml` (and the corresponding `uv.lock` metadata), preventing any future resolver from backtracking to the incompatible v2 series. The lock file has already been pinning `fastmcp==3.1.0` since PR #166, so there is no runtime impact — this is purely a spec/metadata alignment. - `pyproject.toml`: `fastmcp` floor raised to `>=3.0.0` - `uv.lock`: `package.metadata.requires-dist` updated to match; the resolved package entry (`3.1.0`) is unchanged - No upper-bound cap (`<4.0.0`) is set, which is consistent with the project's existing open-ended constraints for all other dependencies

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it is a pure metadata alignment with no functional or runtime impact. - The locked version was already `3.1.0` before this PR; the only change is raising the declared floor to match. Both modified lines are trivially correct, consistent with each other, and have no side-effects on the installed environment. - No files require special attention.

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | pyproject.toml | Single-line change updating the `fastmcp` floor constraint from `>=2.14.0` to `>=3.0.0`, aligning with the already-resolved version in the lock file. | | uv.lock | Auto-generated lock file metadata updated to reflect the new `>=3.0.0` specifier; the resolved `fastmcp` version (3.1.0) was already correct and unchanged. |

"could resolve to" .-> E["fastmcp 2.x\n(incompatible)"] style D fill:#f9d0d0,stroke:#c00 style E fill:#f9d0d0,stroke:#c00 style A fill:#d0f0d0,stroke:#060 style B fill:#d0f0d0,stroke:#060 style C fill:#d0f0d0,stroke:#060 ``` Last reviewed commit: 7d2363e

pullRequest.title: feat(tools): add job IDs, sidebar scrolling, and pagination to search_jobs
pullRequest.body:
Extract job IDs from href attributes (the one thing innerText can't capture), scroll the job sidebar instead of the main page, and paginate through multiple result pages with dynamic offsets. Resolves: #195

Greptile Summary

This PR extends `search_jobs` with job ID extraction from href attributes, sidebar-targeted scrolling, and multi-page pagination. The implementation is sound: - **Job ID extraction** (`_extract_job_ids`): Successfully extracts numeric IDs from job card hrefs. Note: Queries the full document, which may capture IDs from recommendation rails outside the main search results list. This is an existing architectural concern and is covered by prior review comments. - **Sidebar scrolling** (`scroll_job_sidebar`): New utility that locates the scrollable sidebar container via DOM traversal and iteratively loads content. Well-structured with distinct sentinel values (-2, -1, 0, positive) for different failure/success paths. - **Pagination logic**: Uses LinkedIn's "Page X of Y" indicator to cap iteration, with an early-stop guard when no new IDs appear. The `total_pages_queried` boolean ensures the pagination state is read once per search. - **URL construction**: Properly encodes keywords and location with `quote_plus`. The `max_pages` range (1–10) is enforced at both the tool boundary (FastMCP `Field` constraint) and within the loop guard. - **Rate limit handling**: Detects and retries rate-limited pages with a backoff delay. Gracefully returns sentinels for rate limits and soft blocks. **Test coverage**: Comprehensive `TestSearchJobs` suite validates pagination mechanics, deduplication, early-stop conditions, and zero-results capture.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Code is correct and safe to merge. Core pagination, scrolling, and ID extraction logic is sound with comprehensive test coverage and proper error handling. - The implementation correctly handles pagination with early-stop guards, properly encodes URL parameters, and manages rate-limiting with backoff retry. The `scroll_job_sidebar` utility is well-structured with distinct sentinel values for error paths and proper logging. Test suite is thorough, covering pagination mechanics, deduplication, zero-results handling, and URL redirect guards. Minor architectural concerns (whole-DOM ID query scope, class-based pagination selector) are pre-existing and already tracked in prior review comments—not new issues introduced by this PR. No blocking defects remain after code verification. - No files require special attention. All identified concerns are pre-existing architectural issues tracked in prior reviews.

Sequence Diagram

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Tool as search_jobs tool participant Extractor as LinkedInExtractor participant Page as Playwright Page participant LinkedIn as LinkedIn Tool->>Extractor: search_jobs(keywords, max_pages) Extractor->>Extractor: _build_job_search_url() loop for page_num in range(max_pages) Note over Extractor: Guard: break if page_num >= total_pages Extractor->>Extractor: _extract_search_page(url) Extractor->>Page: page.goto(url) Page->>LinkedIn: HTTP GET LinkedIn-->>Page: HTML Page-->>Extractor: domcontentloaded Extractor->>Page: wait_for_selector("main") Extractor->>Page: handle_modal_close() Extractor->>Page: scroll_job_sidebar() Page-->>Extractor: scrollCount sentinel Extractor->>Page: evaluate(main.innerText) Page-->>Extractor: raw text Extractor-->>Extractor: strip_linkedin_noise(text) Note over Extractor: Break if empty or RATE_LIMITED_MSG alt total_pages not yet queried Extractor->>Page: evaluate(.jobs-search-pagination__page-state) Page-->>Extractor: Page X of Y or null end Note over Extractor: Break if URL not /jobs/search/ Extractor->>Page: evaluate(querySelectorAll a href jobs view) Page-->>Extractor: job IDs list Note over Extractor: Break if no new IDs Extractor->>Extractor: update seen_ids and page_texts end Extractor-->>Tool: url + sections + job_ids ```

Comments Outside Diff (2)

 1. General comment **`sort_by` CSV normalization is misleading** `_normalize_csv` is used for `sort_by`, but LinkedIn's `sortBy` query parameter only accepts a single value. Passing `sort_by="date,relevance"` would silently produce `sortBy=DD,R`, which LinkedIn would either ignore or treat unpredictably. Consider either restricting the parameter to a single value (no `_normalize_csv`) or adding a guard: ```python if sort_by: normalized = _normalize_csv(sort_by, _SORT_BY_MAP) params += f"&sortBy={normalized.split(',')[0]}" # take only the first ``` Alternatively, document explicitly that only a single `sort_by` value is supported so callers aren't surprised.
Prompt To Fix With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: linkedin_mcp_server/scraping/extractor.py Line: 248-250 Comment: **`sort_by` CSV normalization is misleading** `_normalize_csv` is used for `sort_by`, but LinkedIn's `sortBy` query parameter only accepts a single value. Passing `sort_by="date,relevance"` would silently produce `sortBy=DD,R`, which LinkedIn would either ignore or treat unpredictably. Consider either restricting the parameter to a single value (no `_normalize_csv`) or adding a guard: ```python if sort_by: normalized = _normalize_csv(sort_by, _SORT_BY_MAP) params += f"&sortBy={normalized.split(',')[0]}" # take only the first ``` Alternatively, document explicitly that only a single `sort_by` value is supported so callers aren't surprised. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````
2. `tests/test_scraping.py`, line 887-921 ([link](https://github.com/stickerdaniel/linkedin-mcp-server/blob/0236331a78f59e7282b3355a55976c745c9bb41a/tests/test_scraping.py#L887-L921)) **`test_page_texts_joined_with_separator` omits `job_ids` assertion** The test verifies that `page_texts` are joined correctly with `\n---\n` and checks `mock_extract.await_count == 2`, but it never asserts on `result["job_ids"]`. An accidental bug that dropped IDs from one of the pages (e.g. resetting `all_job_ids` at the start of each loop iteration) would be invisible to this test. Adding the assertion makes the test also guard against regressions in ID accumulation across pages: ```python assert "\n---\n" in result["sections"]["search_results"] assert "Page 1 content" in result["sections"]["search_results"] assert "Page 2 content" in result["sections"]["search_results"] assert result["job_ids"] == ["100", "200"] # IDs accumulated from both pages assert mock_extract.await_count == 2 ```
Prompt To Fix With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: tests/test_scraping.py Line: 887-921 Comment: **`test_page_texts_joined_with_separator` omits `job_ids` assertion** The test verifies that `page_texts` are joined correctly with `\n---\n` and checks `mock_extract.await_count == 2`, but it never asserts on `result["job_ids"]`. An accidental bug that dropped IDs from one of the pages (e.g. resetting `all_job_ids` at the start of each loop iteration) would be invisible to this test. Adding the assertion makes the test also guard against regressions in ID accumulation across pages: ```python assert "\n---\n" in result["sections"]["search_results"] assert "Page 1 content" in result["sections"]["search_results"] assert "Page 2 content" in result["sections"]["search_results"] assert result["job_ids"] == ["100", "200"] # IDs accumulated from both pages assert mock_extract.await_count == 2 ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

pullRequest.title: fix(scraping): Wait for activity feed content before extracting
pullRequest.body:
Activity feed pages lazy-load post content after tab headers render. Add wait_for_function check and slower scroll params for /recent-activity/ URLs so posts section returns actual content instead of just tab headers. Resolves: #201

Greptile Summary

This PR successfully fixes a race condition in LinkedIn activity-feed extraction. Activity feed pages lazy-load post content _after_ tab headers render, causing `_extract_page_once` to return only empty tab headers. The fix is well-implemented: - Detects activity URLs via `"/recent-activity/" in url` - Adds a `wait_for_function` gate that polls `main.innerText.length > 200` with a 10-second timeout before scrolling - Adjusts scroll parameters for activity pages (`pause_time=1.0`, `max_scrolls=10`) vs. normal pages (`pause_time=0.5`, `max_scrolls=5`) - Handles timeout gracefully with debug logging and continues with available text The test suite comprehensively covers: - Activity page wait + slow-scroll path - Non-activity fast-scroll path - Timeout-fallback graceful degradation The implementation is minimal, focused, and includes proper error handling with no regressions to existing functionality.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge — the fix is well-scoped, correctly implements async/await patterns, and includes graceful timeout handling with no regressions. - The implementation is correct, minimal, and focused on solving the specific race condition. The logic change (conditional wait + adjusted scroll params) is properly isolated to activity URLs. All error paths are handled gracefully. Tests provide good coverage of the key branches. No functional issues or regressions detected. - No files require special attention.

Sequence Diagram

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller participant LinkedInExtractor participant Page as Patchright Page participant DOM Caller->>LinkedInExtractor: _extract_page_once(url) LinkedInExtractor->>Page: goto(url, wait_until="domcontentloaded") Page-->>LinkedInExtractor: loaded LinkedInExtractor->>Page: wait_for_selector("main") Page-->>LinkedInExtractor: main found LinkedInExtractor->>Page: handle_modal_close() Page-->>LinkedInExtractor: done alt "/recent-activity/" in url LinkedInExtractor->>Page: wait_for_function(main.innerText.length > 200, timeout=10s) alt Content loads in time DOM-->>Page: innerText > 200 chars Page-->>LinkedInExtractor: resolved else Timeout Page-->>LinkedInExtractor: PlaywrightTimeoutError LinkedInExtractor->>LinkedInExtractor: log debug, continue end LinkedInExtractor->>Page: scroll_to_bottom(pause=1.0, max=10) else Other URL LinkedInExtractor->>Page: scroll_to_bottom(pause=0.5, max=5) end LinkedInExtractor->>Page: evaluate(main.innerText) Page-->>LinkedInExtractor: raw text LinkedInExtractor->>LinkedInExtractor: strip_linkedin_noise(raw) LinkedInExtractor-->>Caller: cleaned text ``` Last reviewed commit: b76460d

pullRequest.title: fix(scraping): wait for search results content before extracting
pullRequest.body:
Search results pages load a placeholder first, then fill in the actual results via JavaScript. The extractor reads innerText before content loads, causing search_people to return empty sections ~40% of the time. Add wait_for_function for /search/results/ URLs, same pattern as the activity feed fix in #203. Includes 3 tests mirroring TestActivityFeedExtraction. Resolves: #224 Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a race condition where `search_people` returned empty results ~40% of the time because `_extract_page_once` read `innerText` before search results finished rendering via JavaScript. The fix adds a `wait_for_function` call for `/search/results/` URLs that blocks until `main.innerText.length > 100`, matching the pattern established in #203 for activity feeds. Three tests covering the happy path, non-search URL exclusion, and timeout graceful degradation are included. - Adds search results content wait in `extractor.py:_extract_page_once` with a 10s timeout and `> 100` character threshold - Gracefully degrades on timeout — logs a debug message and proceeds with whatever text is available - Tests mirror the existing `TestActivityFeedExtraction` class structure

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it adds a well-scoped wait condition that gracefully degrades on timeout, with good test coverage. - Score of 4 reflects a clean, focused bug fix that follows an established pattern in the codebase (activity feed wait from #203). The logic is correct, the timeout handling is graceful, and three tests cover the key scenarios. Minor deduction because the threshold (100 characters) is a heuristic that could potentially still race in edge cases, though this matches the existing approach. - No files require special attention.

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | linkedin_mcp_server/scraping/extractor.py | Adds a `wait_for_function` block for `/search/results/` URLs that waits until `main.innerText.length > 100`, mirroring the existing activity feed pattern. Clean, correct, and consistent with existing code. | | tests/test_scraping.py | Adds 3 tests for search results wait behavior (wait triggered, not triggered for non-search URLs, graceful timeout). Mirrors the existing `TestActivityFeedExtraction` pattern. |

Sequence Diagram

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller as search_people() participant EPO as _extract_page_once() participant Page as Patchright Page Caller->>EPO: URL = /search/results/people/... EPO->>Page: navigate to URL EPO->>Page: detect_rate_limit() EPO->>Page: wait_for_selector("main", 5s) EPO->>Page: handle_modal_close() Note over EPO: is_activity = false Note over EPO: is_search = true EPO->>Page: wait_for_function(main.innerText > 100, 10s) alt Content loads in time Page-->>EPO: resolved else Timeout Page-->>EPO: PlaywrightTimeoutError Note over EPO: Log debug, continue end EPO->>Page: scroll_to_bottom(pause=0.5, max=5) EPO->>Page: evaluate (extract innerText) Page-->>EPO: raw text + references EPO-->>Caller: ExtractedSection ``` Last reviewed commit: 46a9fbe (2/5) Greptile learns from your feedback when you react with thumbs up/down!

pullRequest.title: fix(login): pass --chrome-path to BrowserManager during --login
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

- `--chrome-path` was ignored during `--login` because `setup.py:interactive_login()` created `BrowserManager` without passing `executable_path`
- Read `config.browser.chrome_path` and forward it as `executable_path` to `BrowserManager`, matching the existing server runtime behavior in `drivers/browser.py:_launch_options()`
- Updated tests to mock `get_config` in the setup module

Closes #260

**Prompt:** "The --chrome-path flag is being ignored during --login. Find where launch_persistent_context is called during the --login flow and make sure executable_path gets set from the --chrome-path argument."
## Test plan

- [x] All 289 existing tests pass
- [ ] Run `uv run -m linkedin_mcp_server --login --chrome-path "/path/to/browser"` and verify it launches the specified browser
- [ ] Run `uv run -m linkedin_mcp_server --login` (without `--chrome-path`) and verify default Chromium still works

pullRequest.title: fix(scraping): Respect --timeout for messaging, extract inbox thread IDs
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

- **#296**: Remove all hardcoded `timeout=5000` from `send_message` and messaging helpers. Playwright calls now fall through to the page-level default set from `BrowserConfig.default_timeout`, making the `--timeout` CLI flag effective for the messaging flow.
- **#297**: Add click-and-capture extraction to `get_inbox` — LinkedIn's conversation sidebar uses JS click handlers (not `<a>` tags), so we click each conversation item and read the SPA URL change to capture thread IDs. Before: 2 references (active conversation only). After: all conversation thread IDs with participant names.
- **#299**: Remove the remaining 10 hardcoded `timeout=5000` from profile scraping, connection flow, modal detection, sidebar profiles, conversation resolution, and job search. Zero `timeout=5000` left in `extractor.py`.
- Add `"conversation"` kind to `classify_link` for `/messaging/thread/` URLs, with proper inbox reference caps and context labels.

## Test plan

- [ ] `uv run pytest` — all 144 tests pass
- [ ] Live: `get_inbox` returns conversation references with thread IDs for all visible conversations
- [ ] Live: `send_message` dry run works with default timeout (no hardcoded 5s override)
- [ ] Live: `get_conversation(thread_id=...)` works with thread IDs from inbox references
- [ ] `--timeout 15000` flag is respected across all scraping flows

Resolves #296, resolves #297, resolves #299

pullRequest.title: fix(scraping): wait for detail panel before extracting experience sections
pullRequest.body:
## Problem

Profile detail pages (`/details/experience/`, `/details/education/`, etc.) silently return no content. The experience section is absent from the scrape result with no error.

**Root cause:** LinkedIn's SPA initially renders sidebar recommendations into `<main>` while the actual section panel loads asynchronously (~1–3s later). The extractor was reading `<main>` before the panel arrived, hitting the `"More profiles for you"` noise marker at position 0, truncating to empty string, and silently dropping the section.

Reproducer:
```
get_person_profile(linkedin_username="<anyone>", sections="experience")
# → sections: { main_profile: "..." }  ← experience missing entirely
```

## Fix

Add a `wait_for_function` guard for `/details/` pages — same pattern already used for `/recent-activity/` and `/search/results/`. Polls until `<main>` stops leading with the known sidebar placeholders before proceeding with scroll + extract.

Closes #335

## Synthetic prompt

> In `_extract_page_once` in `extractor.py`, add a `wait_for_function` guard for `/details/` URLs (experience, education, etc.) that polls until `<main>.innerText` no longer starts with `"Load more"`, `"More profiles for you"`, or `"Explore premium profiles"` — mirroring the existing guards for activity and search pages. Update the related unit test whose URL was incorrectly `/details/experience/` and add a new test asserting the wait fires for detail pages.
